story:
Five days since I last saw Mom. Jenine Killian—forty-six, five three, pale brown skin, dark hair thick enough to hide squirrels—always in a rust-colored cardigan three sizes too big. It must’ve been Dad’s. I can’t even tell you how tall he was when he died; the grief is a ghost I can’t touch. I promised I wouldn’t cry in front of her after he passed, but the night I found her on the porch, staring at stars like little chunks of meat in a murky stew, I wrapped my arms around her from behind and broke my promise. Tea tree wash on her skin put me home, even while she stayed far away, eyes fixed upward. I dragged her back to bed across water-damaged boards that kept our small world raised above the dirt. She slept hot; her side warmed the sheets. Most mornings, she rose first and tucked my feet back under the covers. Not this time. No bowl in the sink, no shoes by the door, only my breath and a loose lock daring anyone to walk in—or out.

There’s a thread you feel when someone is gone for good. It tugged me outside. March rain had erased any footprints. I pushed into pines tall as gallows, their tops swallowed by sky. Mom had taught me about rings in trees—years layered around a dark starting point, a small mole in the center. I called her name until it emptied out, Mom! Mom? mom?—a question the trees could not answer. Looking up when I should’ve looked down, I nearly fell into a hole cut into the heart of the woods. Soil slid away. Fifteen feet below, Mom was digging—nails split, dirt packed under them, flinging clumps behind her. Mud caked her from hair to ankles.

“Mom?” She didn’t look up.

“Mom!” The louder I got, the harder she dug.

“I’ll get Dad’s old ladder!” I ran home and wrestled it back through the brush. Hours passed. She was deeper, eyes raw and red in the high sun. The hole had made a bubble around us; the rest of the world stayed out. “Did I do something wrong?” I asked into the grit. Her breath rasped. “Not…you,” she said, and kept clawing. Evening slid down the trees. I grabbed her leg; she kicked free, her thin calves working like oars. I remembered bathtub peekaboo when I was three—Mom’s face popping out of bubbles, my world reappearing with it. Dirt isn’t bubbles. If something popped out of this ground, I knew it wouldn’t be her.

I crawled in after her. The earth gave way. Cold darkness hit like meat-locker air. I heard shallow gasps and dry coughs, bodies moving in a wide tunnel ribbed with roots. Solemn adults fell through to join the slow current. Dug-out faces, bony arms, feet heavy with mud. “Mom!” I shouted into a sea of strangers and lost her. Children moved among them, small heads brushing my elbows—little ones grabbing up for hands that didn’t reach down, older kids hunched into their coats, searching without much hope. Time slowed, or broke.

I found her at last standing near the wall, a blankness in her eyes I’d seen before dinner when she set a third plate by instinct. A second later she’d fix it and smile as if nothing had slipped. Down here, nothing snapped back. I’d come for my mother and found a husk pulled by something behind her, something older than I am. This was the woman who said goodbye without words, who raised me alone while a shadow made a home under her eyes. The past had its hands in her shoulders and its fingers in the dirt. We walk where it leads until it swallows us. If you want to know how a child will turn out, look at the parent. I looked. We all know where the path goes, even when we try not to.
